skill.name: mcp-server
skill.description: Generic MCP (Model Context Protocol) server development patterns. Provides reusable architecture and best practices for building MCP servers that expose any domain-specific operations as tools for AI agents. Framework-agnostic implementation supporting async operations, error handling, and enterprise-grade features.
skill.summary: Generic MCP (Model Context Protocol) server development patterns. Provides reusable architecture and...
skill.author: Azeem-2

security_audit.summary: Static command execution and script findings are mostly false positives caused by Markdown code fences and Python import examples. The skill is legitimate MCP server guidance, but its sample database layer contains unsafe SQL string construction and a permission stub that always allows access, so publication should include a security warning.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe SQL Identifier Interpolation in Examples
medium_findings.description: The sample database manager builds SQL with interpolated table names and order clauses. Query values are parameterized, but identifiers and ordering input can still create SQL injection risk if developers reuse the pattern with untrusted values.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly inserts table names, order_by, and limit into SQL strings. It is documentation sample code, so the marketplace risk is moderate rather than a direct exploit in an installed executable.

medium_findings.title: Permission Check Stub Allows All Access
medium_findings.description: The base tool permission helper always returns true. This is acceptable as a placeholder only if implementers replace it, but unsafe if copied into a production MCP server.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The implementation explicitly returns True for permission checks. The surrounding comments imply it is a placeholder, but the example can still mislead implementers.

medium_findings.title: Environment and Database Connection Handling
medium_findings.description: The examples read Redis and database URLs from environment variables and include a local placeholder database URL. This is normal server configuration, but it requires secret handling guidance.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment reads are legitimate for MCP server configuration and no exfiltration was found. The placeholder credential string can still encourage poor secret hygiene if copied unchanged.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged backticks as Ruby or shell execution, but the locations are Markdown code fences around Python examples. No executable shell command was found at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown fences and prose delimiters, not Ruby backticks or shell execution. The file is a skill document containing code examples.

low_findings.title: Python Import Block Misclassified as Dynamic Script Loading
low_findings.description: The dynamic import finding points to a Python multi-line import from mcp.types. It is static example code, not JavaScript dynamic import execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text is a normal Python import statement inside a Markdown code block. No dynamic import expression or runtime loader is present.

low_findings.title: MD5 Used for Cache Key Naming
low_findings.description: The examples use MD5 to create cache keys from JSON arguments. This is not password hashing or cryptographic verification, but safer non-cryptographic naming should be documented.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The MD5 call is real, but it is used for cache key derivation rather than security-sensitive hashing. The risk is low unless implementers reuse it for trust decisions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Raw SQL Construction from Dynamic Identifiers
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill demonstrates dynamic SQL assembly for table names and ordering. Implementations should whitelist identifiers and avoid exposing those fields directly to tool callers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicitly present and can become exploitable in a copied implementation. The finding is bounded because the skill itself is documentation.

content.user_title: Build MCP Servers with Reusable Patterns
content.value_statement: Building an MCP server can require repeated work for tools, data access, validation, and tests. This skill provides reusable Python patterns for Model Context Protocol servers with database-backed tools.
actual_capabilities: Explains a reusable Python architecture for MCP servers.
Shows patterns for registering MCP tools and resources.
Provides database backend examples for PostgreSQL, MongoDB, SQLAlchemy, and Redis.
Includes generic CRUD and bulk operation tool patterns.
Demonstrates a task management MCP server built from the shared patterns.
Includes testing patterns for CRUD tools and MCP server behavior.
limitations: It is a documentation skill, not a packaged runnable server.
Some security-sensitive examples require hardening before production use.
Database schemas and permission logic must be adapted for each project.
It assumes Python and async server development experience.

use_cases.title: Create a Domain MCP Server
use_cases.description: Design a new MCP server that exposes domain operations as AI-accessible tools.

use_cases.title: Add Database-Backed Tools
use_cases.description: Use the database patterns to connect MCP tools to PostgreSQL, MongoDB, or SQLAlchemy.

use_cases.title: Prototype Agent Workflows
use_cases.description: Build a task-oriented MCP example that Claude, Codex, or Claude Code can call during development.

prompt_templates.title: Plan My MCP Server
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline a Python MCP server for my domain. Include tools, resources, storage choices, and validation needs.

prompt_templates.title: Design Tool Handlers
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design MCP tool handlers for these operations: [operations]. Include input schemas and error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Adapt Database Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to adapt the database integration pattern for [database]. Include connection lifecycle, queries, and testing notes.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my MCP server design for production readiness. Focus on permissions, SQL safety, rate limits, logging, and tests.

output_examples.input: Plan an MCP server for project task tracking.
output_examples.output: A server design with task tools, resource endpoints, validation rules, database choices, and test areas.

output_examples.input: Add database-backed CRUD tools for customer records.
output_examples.output: A tool plan covering create, read, list, update, delete, bulk operations, permission checks, and storage concerns.

output_examples.input: Review this MCP architecture for production gaps.
output: Permission checks need concrete role logic.
Secrets should come from managed configuration.
Integration tests should cover authorization failures.

best_practices: Treat the code as a starting pattern and harden it before production.
Whitelist table names, sort fields, and exposed operations for every tool.
Replace placeholder permission checks with project-specific authorization logic.
anti_patterns: Do not copy the sample database URL or placeholder credentials into production.
Do not expose raw query controls directly to AI tool callers.
Do not rely on the default permission stub for real user data.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps design Python MCP servers that expose domain operations as AI-callable tools.

faq.question: Does it include a complete application?
faq.answer: No. It provides architecture, examples, and patterns that developers adapt to their project.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use the resulting server?
faq.answer: The patterns target MCP clients, including Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: What databases are covered?
faq.answer: The examples discuss PostgreSQL, MongoDB, SQLAlchemy, Redis caching, and Redis rate limiting.

faq.question: Is the sample code production ready?
faq.answer: No. Developers should harden SQL construction, permissions, secrets, logging, and tests.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: Some examples use dynamic SQL strings and placeholder permission logic that need safer production implementations.
